era_context: Early Dynastic Period I in Mesopotamia (c. 3150–2900 BCE). Egypt in the Early Dynastic Period (c. 3100–2686 BCE), with the Old Kingdom beginning around 2686 BCE. The Indus Valley Civilization in its early phases. Writing systems emerging in multiple regions. City-states consolidating power in Mesopotamia and Egypt.

events.title: Early Dynastic Period I consolidation in Mesopotamia
events.description: During this period, Sumerian city-states such as Uruk, Lagash, Umma, and Ur were consolidating territorial control and developing increasingly complex administrative structures. Archaeological evidence shows expansion of temple complexes and early palace structures. This era marks the transition from prehistoric settlement to organized city-state governance, though specific events for 3140 BCE are not individually documented.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Uruk, Lagash, and other Sumerian sites; Sumerian King List (later compilation)

events.certainty_note: No specific events are dated to 3140 BCE. This entry describes the broader era context. The Early Dynastic Period I is archaeologically attested but individual years are not precisely documented.

events.title: Egyptian Early Dynastic Period development
events.description: Egypt during this period was in the Early Dynastic Period, with the unification of Upper and Lower Egypt traditionally dated to c. 3100 BCE (though this date is debated). By 3140 BCE, Egypt was likely still in the process of regional consolidation, with competing polities in the Nile Valley. Tomb evidence and early inscriptions show increasing administrative complexity.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Abydos, Saqqara, and other Egyptian sites; Egyptian King Lists (later compilation)

events.certainty_note: Egyptian chronology for this period is uncertain by ±50–100 years depending on the chronological system used (high, middle, or low chronology). No specific events are documented for 3140 BCE.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional date of Egyptian unification (c. 3100 BCE) is debated by modern scholars. Some argue for dates as late as 3000 BCE or even later. The Sumerian King List, a primary source for Mesopotamian chronology, is now understood to be a literary and political document rather than a reliable historical chronicle, with legendary and mythological rulers mixed with historical ones. No specific events are reliably dated to 3140 BCE in any tradition.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 3140 BCE is almost entirely archaeological. Written records are minimal or absent for this specific year. Egyptian and Mesopotamian chronologies for this period depend on astronomical calculations, radiocarbon dating, and stratigraphic analysis, all of which carry significant uncertainty. The Sumerian King List and Egyptian King Lists are later compilations with ideological purposes and should not be treated as reliable year-by-year records. Bias toward Mesopotamian and Egyptian sources means other regions are virtually undocumented.
